one_line_takeaway: IKEA shows that a RAG knowledge base can be stealthily reconstructed using only benign, natural queries -- bypassing input/output defenses that stop prompt-injection and jailbreak extraction -- yielding a usable pirated substitute RAG, and only a utility-destroying retrieval-perturbation defense stops it.
key_finding: On LLaMA+MPNet, under the input-ensemble defense both baselines are fully blocked (EE=ASR=0 on all three datasets) while IKEA still achieves EE/ASR of 0.88/0.92 (HealthCareMagic), 0.65/0.77 (HarryPotter), and 0.56/0.59 (Pokemon). Under output-level content detection IKEA holds (e.g. 0.85/0.91 on HealthCareMagic) while RAG-Thief/DGEA collapse. The best-case extraction reported (LLaMA+BGE, no defense) is EE=0.91, ASR=0.96 on HealthCareMagic. Notably IKEA's CRR (literal overlap ~0.2-0.3) is deliberately LOWER than baselines' (which reach ~0.8-0.9 CRR under no defense) because IKEA extracts semantic knowledge, not verbatim text -- yet its SS stays high. The substitute RAG built from IKEA extractions (Pokemon, 512-round extraction, both defenses) reaches Acc 0.43 / Rouge 0.19 / Sim 0.33 under input-ensemble vs ~0 for both baselines, and IKEA's extracted-knowledge QA/MCQ (Table 7) approaches origin-context quality (e.g. HealthCare Acc 0.93, HarryPotter 0.94, Pokemon 0.92). The proposed adaptive defense (irrelevant-doc replacement) does degrade IKEA (Pokemon EE falls to 0.12-0.22, substitute Acc to 0.00) but simultaneously destroys benign RAG utility (Acc 0.00, Rouge ~0.00), which the authors argue makes it impractical.
core_contribution: The paper introduces IKEA (Implicit Knowledge Extraction Attack), the first framework to extract a RAG system's private knowledge base using only benign, natural-looking queries rather than prompt injection or jailbreak prompts. Unlike prior verbatim-extraction attacks that trigger detectable input/output patterns, IKEA never asks the model to reproduce documents; instead it drives 'anchor concepts' (topic-related keywords) to progressively explore the RAG's knowledge via two mechanisms: (1) Experience Reflection (ER) Sampling, which maintains a query-response history and down-weights anchor concepts that previously yielded unrelated or refused answers, and (2) Trust Region Directed Mutation (TRDM), which mutates anchor concepts under a similarity-constrained 'trust region' whose radius scales with the query-response semantic distance, to cover unexplored regions of the embedding space. The paper's own contribution is IKEA plus the associated threat framing; prompt-injection (RAG-Thief) and jailbreak (DGEA) methods are cited baselines, not this paper's work.
threat_model: Black-box setting: the attacker interacts only through the RAG system's input-output interface, behaving as a normal user (issue queries, receive responses, store history). The attacker knows only the public, non-sensitive domain topic keyword w_topic and has no knowledge of the LLM, retriever, or embedding model. A weaker variant (Sec 4.6) drops even the topic assumption, recovering a pseudo-topic via 'topic probing' from RAG-vs-non-RAG answer embedding shifts. The deployer is assumed to apply lightweight input-level defenses (intention detection, keyword filtering, defensive instructions) and output-level defense (content/Rouge-L overlap detection). Attacker goal: extract maximum knowledge under a limited query budget, enabling a pirated 'substitute RAG' that infringes copyright/privacy.
limitations: The authors' own adaptive defense (replacing a fraction of top-K retrievals with least-relevant documents) substantially reduces IKEA's effectiveness, so the attack is not robust to defenders willing to perturb retrieval -- the paper only rebuts this by noting the defense also harms utility. Attack assumes a semantically centered, single-domain RAG corpus (validated via t-SNE in Appendix B.5); effectiveness on heterogeneous/multi-topic corpora is untested. The attack relies on a capable external generator (GPT-4o) and a query budget of 256-512 rounds; results are on three English datasets only. IKEA also incurs higher query-token cost (23.68K vs 14.49K for RAG-Thief on Pokemon), though lower total attack-token cost and wall-clock time. CRR is intrinsically low, so the attack does not recover exact copyrighted text, only high-fidelity semantic content.

datasets_used: Pokemon (tungdop2, ~1.27k rows; used for substitute-RAG eval on 1000 entries)
20 second-level Wikipedia categories (seed set for topic-probing in weaker-assumption setting)
quantitative_results: LLaMA+MPNet, Input-Ensemble defense: IKEA EE/ASR = 0.88/0.92 (HealthCareMagic), 0.65/0.77 (HarryPotter), 0.56/0.59 (Pokemon); RAG-Thief and DGEA = 0/0 on all three
LLaMA+BGE, No Defense: IKEA best extraction EE=0.91, ASR=0.96 (HealthCareMagic)
IKEA CRR deliberately low (~0.18-0.33) vs baselines up to 0.90-0.98 CRR under no defense; IKEA SS remains high (~0.59-0.79)
Substitute RAG (Pokemon, 512-round, both defenses), Input-Ensemble: IKEA Acc/Rouge/Sim = 0.43/0.19/0.33 vs RAG-Thief 0/0/0.03 and DGEA 0/0/0.04
Extracted-knowledge QA/MCQ (Table 7, Input-Ensemble): IKEA Acc = 0.93/0.94/0.92 (HealthCare/HarryPotter/Pokemon) vs baselines ~0.44/0.63/0.17
Weaker (unknown-topic) assumption under input-ensemble: IKEA EE/ASR = 0.83/0.92 (Health), 0.65/0.77 (HarryPotter), 0.55/0.58 (Pokemon); Topic SS 0.89/1.00/0.79
Adaptive defense on Pokemon: IKEA EE drops from 0.61 (no defense) to 0.13/0.12/0.22 at replacement ratios 0.1/0.3/0.5, but benign utility Acc also drops to 0.00
Token cost (Pokemon): IKEA 23.68K query tokens (vs 14.49K RAG-Thief, 17.93K DGEA), 208.74K attack tokens (vs 233.91K RAG-Thief), extraction time 5220s (vs 6012s / 6636s)

Headline abstract claims: surpasses baselines by >80% extraction efficiency and >90% attack success rate; extracts >91% of text chunks at 96% success; substitute RAG outperforms baselines by >40% MCQ accuracy and >30% QA similarity
baselines_compared: RAG-Thief (Jiang et al. 2024) -- prompt-injection / agent-based verbatim extraction
DGEA (Cohen et al. 2024) -- jailbreak-based RAG extraction
Reference conditions for utility: Origin (ground-truth chunks) and Empty (no context) knowledge bases
Pure-LLM-exploration (no ER/TRDM optimization) ablation baseline (Appendix B.4)
